)\n145\t        return self.config\n146\t\n147\t    async def check_topic_state(self, rule: MonitoringRule) -> Optional[dict]:\n148\t        \"\"\"\n149\t        Check the current state of a topic.\n150\t\n151\t        Uses the canonical fetch stack to get current state for the topic.\n152\t        \"\"\"\n153\t        import uuid\n154\t\n155\t        # Map intent_type to canonical      intent_type=fetch_intent_type,\n161\t            intent_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),\n162\t            session_id=\"monitoring\",\n163\t            context=FetchContext(\n164\t                project_slug=rule.project_slug,\n165\t                # Other context fields can be added as needed\n166\t            ),\n167\t        )\n168\t\n169\t        # Execute fetch\n170\t        fetch_result {})\n175\t\n176\t        if coverage.get(\"success_rate\", 0) > 0:\n177\t            # Extract successful source data\n178\t            sources = result_dict.get(\"sources\", {})\n179\t            state_data = {\n180\t                \"project_slug\": rule.project_slug,\n181\t                \"intent_type\": rule.intent_type,\n182\t                \"sources\": sources,\n183\t                \"coverage\": coverage,\n184\t            }\n185\t\n186\t            # Apply filters if specified\n187\t            if rule.filters:\n188\t                # Filter on individual source data\n189\t                filtered_sources = {}\n190\t                for source_name, source_data in sources.items():\n191\t                    if isinstance(source_data, dict) and source_data.get(\"status\") == \"success\":\n192\t                        data = source_data.get(\"data\", {})\n193\t                        if self._passes_filters(data, rule.filters):\n194\t                            filtered_sources[source_name] = source_data\n195\t\n196\t                if not filtered_sources:\n197\t                    # All sources were filtered\n198\t                    logger.debug(f\"State for {rule.topic_id} filtered by {rule.filters}\")\n199\t                    return None\n200\t\n201\t                state_data[\"sources\"] = filtered_sources\n202\t\n203\t            return state_data\n204\t        else:\n205\t            logger.warning(f\"Fetch failed for {rule.topic_id}: no successful sources\")\n206\t            return None\n207\t\n208\t    def _passes_filters(self, state_data: dict, filters: list[str]) -> bool:\n209\t        \"\"\"\n210\t        Check if state data passes all filters.\n211\t\n212\t        Filters are simple expressions like \"phase!=Running\" or \"restarts>0\".\n213\t        Returns False if any filter fails.\n214\t        \"\"\"\n215\t        for filter_expr in filters:\n216\t            if not self._evaluate_filter(state_data, filter_expr):\n217\t                return False\n218\t        return True\n219\t\n220\t    def _evaluate_filter(self, state_data: dict, filter_expr: str) -> bool:\n221\t        \"\"\"\n222\t        Evaluate a single filter expression against state data.\n223\t\n224\t        Supports: !=, >, <, >=, <=, ==\n225\t        Examples: \"phase!=Running\", \"restarts>0\", \"ready!=1/1\"\n226\t        \"\"\"\n227\t        import re\n228\t\n229\t        # Parse filter expression\n230\t        match = re.match(r'(\\w+)(!=|>=|<=|>|<|==)(.+)', filter_expr)\n231\t        if not match:\n232\t            logger.warning(f\"Invalid filter expression: {filter_expr}\")\n233\t            return True\n234\t\n235\t        field_path, op, value = match.groups()\n236\t\n237\t        # Get field value from nested dict\n238\t        field_value = self._get_nested_value(state_data, field_path)\n239\t\n240\t        # Compare\n241\t        if field_value is None:\n242\t            return False\n243\t\n244\t        try:\n245\t            if op == \"!=\":\n246\t                return str(field_value) != value\n247\t            elif op == \"==\":\n248\t                return str(field_value) == value\n249\t            elif op == \">\":\n250\t                return float(field_value) > float(value)\n251\t            elif op == \"<\":\n252\t                return float(field_value) < float(value)\n253\t            elif op == \">=\":\n254\t                return float(field_value) >= float(value)\n255\t            elif op == \"<=\":\n256\t                return float(field_value) <= float(value)\n257\t        except (ValueError, TypeError):\n258\t            # If comparison fails, treat as no match\n259\t            logger.warning(f\"Filter comparison failed: {filter_expr} with value {field_value}\")\n260\t            return False\n261\t\n262\t        return True\n263\t\n264\t    def _get_nested_value(self, data: dict, path: str) -> Any:\n265\t        \"\"\"Get a value from nested dict using dot notation.\"\"\"\n266\t        keys = path.split(\".\")\n267\t        value = data\n268\t        for key in keys:\n269\t            if isinstance(value, dict):\n270\t                value = value.get(key)\n271\t            else:\n272\t                return None\n273\t        return value\n274\t\n275\t    async def detect_state_change(\n276\t        self,\n277\t        rule: MonitoringRule,\n278\t        current_state: dict,\n279\t    ) -> bool:\n280\t        \"\"\"\n281\t        Detect if the state has changed since last check.\n282\t\n283\t        Compares current state with last known state for the topic.\n284\t        Returns True if significant change detected.\n285\t        \"\"\"\n286\t        topic_key = f\"{rule.project_slug}:{rule.intent_type}\"\n287\t\n288\t        if topic_key not in self.last_state:\n289\t            # First check - store state and don't notify\n290\t            self.last_state[topic_key] = current_state\n291\t            return False\n292\t\n293\t        last_state = self.last_state[topic_key]\n294\t\n295\t        # Check for state changes based on notification threshold\n296\t        if rule.notification_threshold == \"any_change\":\n297\t            # Any field change triggers notification\n298\t            return current_state != last_state\n299\t        elif rule.notification_threshold == \"state_change\":\n300\t            # Only notify if specific state fields change\n301\t            # For status intents, check if phase, status, or health changed\n302\t            state_fields = [\"phase\", \"status\", \"health\", \"ready\", \"sync_status\"]\n303\t            return any(\n304\t                current_state.get(field) != last_state.get(field)\n305\t                for field in state_fields\n306\t            )\n307\t\n308\t        return False\n309\t\n310\t    async def push_monitoring_result(\n311\t        self,\n312\t        rule: MonitoringRule,\n313\t        current_state: dict,\n314\t        session_id: str,\n315\t    ) -> None:\n316\t        \"\"\"\n317\t        Push a monitoring result to the active surface.\n318\t\n319\t        Creates a result in the session store and pushes to appropriate surface.\n320\t        \"\"\"\n321\t        store = get_store()\n322\t\n323\t        # Find or create topic for this monitoring rule\n324\t        topic_id, _ = await store.find_or_create_topic(\n325\t            label=rule.topic_id,\n326\t            session_id=session_id,\n327\t            topic_type=\"project\",\n328\t            project_slugs=[rule.project_slug] if rule.project_slug else None,\n329\t        )\n330\t\n331\t        # Create a monitoring intent\n332\t        intent_id = await store.create_intent(\n333\t            utterance_id=f\"monitoring-{datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()}\",\n334\t            session_id=session_id,\n335\t            project_slug=rule.project_slug,\n336\t            intent_type=rule.intent_type,\n337\t        )\n338\t\n339\t        # Create result with diff info if available\n340\t        topic_key = f\"{rule.project_slug}:{rule.intent_type}\"\n341\t        previous_state = self.last_state.get(topic_key, {})\n342\t\n343\t        result_data = {\n344\t            \"monitoring\": True,\n345\t            \"current_state\": current_state,\n346\t            \"previous_state\": previous_state if previous_state else None,\n347\t            \"rule_filters\": rule.filters,\n348\t        }\n349\t\n350\t        # Add diff if we have previous state\n351\t        if previous_state:\n352\t            result_data[\"diff\"] = self._compute_diff(previous_state, current_state)\n353\t\n354\t        result_id = await store.create_result(\n355\t            intent_id=intent_id,\n356\t            topic_id=topic_id,\n357\t            session_id=session_id,\n358\t            summary=self._generate_summary(rule, current_state, previous_state),\n359\t            data=result_data,\n360\t            urgency=rule.urgency,\n361\t        )\n362\t\n363\t        logger.info(f\"Created monitoring result {result_id} for topic {rule.topic_id}\")\n364\t\n365\t        # Update last state\n366\t        self.last_state[topic_key] = current_state\n367\t\n368\t    def _compute_diff(self, previous: dict, current: dict) -> dict:\n369\t        \"\"\"Compute diff between previous and current state.\"\"\"\n370\t        diff = {}\n371\t\n372\t        for key in set(list(previous.keys()) + list(current.keys())):\n373\t            prev_val = previous.get(key)\n374\t            curr_val = current.get(key)\n375\t\n376\t            if prev_val != curr_val:\n377\t                diff[key] = {\n378\t                    \"from\": prev_val,\n379\t                    \"to\": curr_val,\n380\t                }\n381\t\n382\t        return diff\n383\t\n384\t    def _generate_summary(\n385\t        self,\n386\t        rule: MonitoringRule,\n387\t        current_state:        self._http_client = ClientSession()\n        return self._http_client\n\n    async def load_config(self) -> MonitoringConfig:\n        \"\"\"Load monitoring configuration from YAML file.\"\"\"\n        logger.info(f\"Loading monitoring config from {self.config_path}\")\n        with open(self.config_path, \"r\") as f:\n            data = yaml.safe_load(f)\n\n        # Parse active topics\n        active_topics = []\n        for item in data.get(\"monitoring\", {}).get(\"active_topics\", []):\n            active_topics.append(MonitoringRule(\n                topic_id=item[\"topic_id\"],\n                project_slug=item[\"project_slug\"],\n                intent_type=item[\"intent_type\"],\n                check_interval=item[\"check_interval\"],\n                urgency=item.get(\"urgency\", \"normal\"),\n                filters=item.get(\"filters\", []),\n                notification_threshold=item.get(\"notification_threshold\", \"any_change\"),\n            ))\n\n        # Parse exceptions\n        exceptions = []\n        for item in data.get(\"monitoring\", {}).get(\"exceptions\", []):\n            exceptions.append(ExceptionRule(\n get current state for the topic.\n        \"\"\"\n        import uuid\n\n        # Map intent_type to canonical IntentType\n        fetch_intent_type = INTENT_TYPE_TO_FETCH.get(rule.intent_type, session_id=\"monitoring\",\n            context=FetchContext(\n                project_slug=rule.project_slug,\n                # Other context fields can be added as needed\n            ),\n        )\n\n        # Execute fetch\n        fetch_result = await execute_fetch(request)\n\n        # Convert to dict and check success\n        result_dict = fetch_result.to_dict()\n        coverage =  state_data = {\n                \"project_slug\": rule.project_slug,\n                \"intent_type\": rule.intent_type,\n                \"sources\": sources,\n                \"coverage\":       for source_name, source_data in sources.items():\n                    if isinstance(source_data, dict) and source_data.get(\"status\") == \"success\":\n                        data = source_data.get(\"data\", {})\n                        if self._passes_filters(data, rule.filters):\n                            filtered_sources[source_name] = source_data\n\n                if not filtered_sources:\n                    # All sources were filtered\n                    logger.debug(f\"State for {rule.topic_id} filtered by {rule.filters}\")\n                    return None\n\n                state_data[\"sources\"] = filtered_sources\n\n            return state_data\n        else:\n            logger.warning(f\"Fetch failed for {rule.topic_id}: no successful sources\")\n            return None\n\n    def _passes_filters(self, state_data: dict, filters: list[str]) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Check if state data passes all filters.\n\n        Filters are simple expressions like \"phase!=Running\" or \"restarts>0\".\n        Returns False if any filter fails.\n        \"\"\"\n        for filter_expr in filters:\n            if not self._evaluate_filter(state_data, filter_expr):\n                return False\n        return True\n\n    def _evaluate_filter(self, state_data: dict, filter_expr: str) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Evaluate a single filter expression against state data.\n\n        Supports: !=, >, <, >=, <=, ==\n        Examples: \"phase!=Running\", \"restarts>0\", \"ready!=1/1\"\n        \"\"\"\n        import re\n\n        # Parse filter expression\n        match = re.match(r'(\\w+)(!=|>=|<=|>|<|==)(.+)', filter_expr)\n        if not match:\n            logger.warning(f\"Invalid filter expression: {filter_expr}\")\n            return True\n\n        field_path, op, value = match.groups()\n\n        # Get field value from nested dict\n        field_value = self._get_nested_value(state_data, field_path)\n\n        # Compare\n        if field_value is None:\n            return False\n\n        try:\n            if op == \"!=\":\n                return str(field_value) != value\n            elif op == \"==\":\n                return str(field_value) == value\n            elif op == \">\":\n                return float(field_value) > float(value)\n            elif op == \"<\":\n                return float(field_value) < float(value)\n            elif op == \">=\":\n                return float(field_value) >= float(value)\n            elif op == \"<=\":\n                return float(field_value) <= float(value)\n        except (ValueError, TypeError):\n            # If comparison fails, treat as no match\n            logger.warning(f\"Filter comparison failed: {filter_expr} with value {field_value}\")\n            return False\n\n        return True\n\n    def _get_nested_value(self, data: dict, path: str) -> Any:\n        \"\"\"Get a value from nested dict using dot notation.\"\"\"\n        keys = path.split(\".\")\n        value = data\n        for key in keys:\n            if isinstance(value, dict):\n                value = value.get(key)\n            else:\n                return None\n        return value\n\n    async def detect_state_change(\n        self,\n        rule: MonitoringRule,\n        current_state: dict,\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Detect if the state has changed since last check.\n\n        Compares current state with last known state for the topic.\n        Returns True if significant change detected.\n        \"\"\"\n        topic_key = f\"{rule.project_slug}:{rule.intent_type}\"\n\n        if topic_key not in self.last_state:\n            # First check - store state and don't notify\n            self.last_state[topic_key] = current_state\n            return False\n\n        last_state = self.last_state[topic_key]\n\n        # Check for state changes based on notification threshold\n        if rule.notification_threshold == \"any_change\":\n            # Any field change triggers notification\n            return current_state != last_state\n        elif rule.notification_threshold == \"state_change\":\n            # Only notify if specific state fields change\n            # For status intents, check if phase, status, or health changed\n            state_fields = [\"phase\", \"status\", \"health\", \"ready\", \"sync_status\"]\n            return any(\n                current_state.get(field) != last_state.get(field)\n                for field in state_fields\n            )\n\n        return False\n\n    async def push_monitoring_result(\n        self,\n        rule: MonitoringRule,\n        current_state: dict,\n        session_id: str,\n    ) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Push a monitoring result to the active surface.\n\n        Creates a result in the session store and pushes to appropriate surface.\n        \"\"\"\n        store = get_store()\n\n intent_type=rule.intent_type,\n        )\n\n        # Create result with diff info if available\n        topic_key = f\"{rule.project_slug}:{rule.intent_type}\"\n        previous_state = previous_state else None,\n            \"rule_filters\": rule.filters,\n        }\n\n        # Add diff if we have previous state\n        if previous_state:\n            result_data[\"diff\"] = session_id=session_id,\n            summary=self._generate_summary(rule, current_state, previous_state),\n            data=result_data,\n            urgency=rule.urgency,\n        )\n\n        logger.info(f\"Created monitoring result {result_id} for topic {rule.topic_id}\")\n\n        # Update last state\n        self.last_state[topic_key] = current_state\n\n    def _compute_diff(self, previous: dict, current: dict) -> dict:\n        \"\"\"Compute diff between previous and current state.\"\"\"\n        diff = {}\n\n        for key in set(list(previous.keys()) + list(current.keys())):\n            prev_val = previous.get(key)\n            curr_val = current.get(key)\n\n            if prev_val != curr_val:\n                diff[key] = {\n                    \"from\": prev_val,\n                    \"to\": curr_val,\n                }\n\n        return diff\n\n    def _generate_summary(\n        self,\n        rule: MonitoringRule,\n        current_state: dict,\n        previous_state: Optional[dict],\n    ) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Generate a summary for the monitoring result.\"\"\"\n        if previous_state is None:\n            return f\"Initial state check for {rule.topic_id}\"\n\n        # Check for specific state changes\n        if \"phase\" in current_state:\n            curr_phase = current_state[\"phase\"]\n            prev_phase = previous_state.get(\"phase\")\n            if curr_phase != prev_phase:\n                return f\"{rule.topic_id} phase changed from {prev_phase} to {curr_phase}\"\n\n        if \"sync_status\" in       \"\"\"\n        Monitor a single topic on its check interval.\n\n        Runs in a loop until cancelled.\n        \"\"\"\n        logger.info(f\"Starting monitor for topic {rule.topic_id}     if current_state:\n                    # Detect change\n                    has_change = await self.detect_state_change(rule, current_state)\n\n                    if has_change:\n           # Update last state even if no change (keeps us synced)\n                        topic_key = f\"{rule.project_slug}:{rule.intent_type}\"\n                        self.last_state[topic_key] = \"\"\"Stop ambient monitoring.\"\"\"\n        logger.info(\"Stopping ambient monitoring service\")\n        self.running = False\n\n        # Cancel all monitor tasks\n        for task in - Result delivery when no other surface is available\n    - Exception push for critical/high urgency items\n    \"\"\"\n\n    # telegram-claude-bridge endpoint (Tailscale mesh)\n    # Configurable via ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL env var\n    DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL = \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\"\n    # Rate limit for repeated failure logs (5 minutes)\n    FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300\n\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        import os\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n        )\n        # Track bridge reachability state\n        self._is_reachable = None  # None = unknown, True = reachable, False = unreachable\n        self._last_failure_logged = None  # Track when we last logged a failure\n        self._failure_count = 0  # Track total failures\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False  # Track if we've logged the first failure after startup\n\n    async def send_message(\n        self,\n        chat_id: int | str,\n        message: str,\n        parse_mode: str = \"HTML\",\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a message to a Telegram chat.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID (int or str)\n            message: Message text to send\n            parse_mode: Parse mode for formatting (Markdown, HTML, etc.)\n\n        Returns:\n            True if successful, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n                # telegram-claude-bridge proxy API uses /send endpoint\n                # Contract: {chat_id, text, parse_mode?, thread_id?, reply_to_message_id?}\n                response = await client.post(\n                    f\"{self.bridge_url}/send\",\n                    json={\n                        \"chat_id\": int(chat_id) if isinstance(chat_id, str) else chat_id,\n                        \"text\": message,\n                        \"parse_mode\": parse_mode,\n                    },\n                    timeout=10.0,\n                )\n\n                if response.status_code == 200:\n                    logger.info(f\"Sent Telegram message to chat {chat_id}\")\n                    self._is_reachable = True  # Update reachability state\n                    return True\n                else:\n                    error_msg = f\"status {response.status_code} - {response.text}\"\n                    self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n                    return False\n\n        except httpx.RequestError as e:\n            error_msg = f\"request error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n        except Exception as e:\n            error_msg = f\"unexpected error: {e}\"\n            self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n            return False\n\n    async def send_result(self, chat_id: int | str, result: dict) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send a structured result to Telegram.\n\n        Args:\n            chat_id: Telegram chat ID\n            result: Result dict with keys: summary, urgency, data\n\n        Formats the result as a readable message and sends it.\n        \"\"\"\n        message = self._format_result_message(result)\n        return await self.send_message(chat_id, message)\n\n    async def send_exception(\n        self,\n        session_id: str,\n        exception: dict,\n    ) -> bool:\n        \"\"\"\n        Send an exception to Telegram for human attention.\n\n        NOTE: This method requires a session→telegram_chat_id mapping which is not\n        currently implemented. telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture\n        (manages sessions internally per forum topic), not push-based message delivery.\n        \"\"\"\n        logger.warning(\n            f\"send_exception() called for session {session_id} - \"\n            f\"session→telegram_chat mapping not implemented. \"\n            f\"telegram-claude-bridge uses pull-based architecture.\"\n        )\n        return False\n\n    async def send_workload_summary(\n        self,\n        session_id: str,\n        summary: dict,\n  self._is_reachable = is_available\n                return is_available\n        except Exception:\n            self._is_reachable = False\n            return False\n\n    def get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n        \"\"\"\n        Get the current bridge status.\n\n        Returns:\n            Dict with keys:\n            - reachable: bool or None (None = unknown yet)\n            - bridge_url: str\n            - failure_count: int\n        \"\"\"\n        return {\n            \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n            \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n            \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n        }\n\n    def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n        \"\"\"Handle a send failure - log warning only on the first failure after startup.\n\n        Logs a WARNING only for the very first failure after startup.\n        All subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n\n        Args:\n            error_context: Details about the error (status code, error message, etc.)\n        \"\"\"\n        self._is_reachable = False\n        self._failure_count += 1\n        now = datetime.now()\n\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure after startup - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n            self._last_failure_logged = now\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failures - log at DEBUG level to avoid spam\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n\n    def _format_result_message(self, result: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format a result as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        summary = result.get(\"summary\", \"Result available\")\n        urgency = result.get(\"urgency\", \"normal\")\n\n        emoji_map = {\n            \"critical\": \"🚨\",\n            \"high\": \"⚠️\",\n            \"normal\": \"📌\",\n            \"low\": {body}\")\n\n        return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n    def _format_exception_message(self, exception: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format an exception as a prominent Telegram message.\"\"\"\n lines.append(\"\")\n        lines.append(\"Reply with your choice or type a custom response.\")\n\n        return \"\\n\".join(lines)\n\n    def _format_workload_summary(self, summary: dict) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format a workload summary as a Telegram message.\"\"\"\n        pending = summary.get(\"pending_intents\", 0)\n        new_results = summary.get(\"new_results\", 0)\n        exceptions = intents: {pending}\")\n\n        if new_results > 0:\n            lines.append(f\"📌 New results: {new_results}\")\n\n        if exceptions > 0:\n            lines.append(f\"🚨 Unresolved exceptions: {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_500ef4ee088242f98e313592","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure State Storage Design\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document evaluates storage options for the first-failure tracking state in the async FastAPI application and documents the recommended approach with rationale.\n6\t\n7\t## Design Requirements\n8\t\n9\t### Functional Requirements\n10\t1. State must track first-failure occurrence across application lifetime\n11\t2. State must survive from application startup until shutdown\n12\t3. Only ONE first-failure event should be detected per startup sequence\n13\t4. State must be accessible to all `send_message()` calls\n14\t\n15\t### Non-Functional Requirements\n16\t1. **Thread-safety**: Multiple concurrent async requests must not corrupt state\n17\t2. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram sends)\n18\t3. **Lifecycle clarity**: Clear initialization and reset behavior\n19\t4. **Simplicity**: Easy to understand, test, and maintain\n20\t\n21\t## Storage Options Evaluation\n22\t\n23\t### Option 1: Module-Level Variable\n24\t\n25\t**Approach:**\n26\t```python\n27\t# In src/telegram/fallback.py\n28\t_first_failure_state = {\n29\t    \"has_logged_first_failure\": False,\n30\t    \"failure_count\": 0,\n31\t    \"first_failure_timestamp\": None,\n32\t    \"last_failure_timestamp\": None,\n33\t}\n34\t```\n35\t\n36\t**Pros:**\n37\t- Simple to understand\n38\t- Accessible from anywhere in the module\n39\t- No instance overhead\n40\t\n41\t**Cons:**\n42\t- ❌ **Not thread-safe**: Concurrent async coroutines can race on check-and-set\n43\t- ❌ **Hard to test**: Global state pollutes tests; requires manual reset\n44\t- ❌ **No encapsulation**: Any code can mutate state directly\n45\t- ❌ **No lock integration**: Would need a separate global lock object\n46\t\n47\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Not thread-safe in async context\n48\t\n49\t---\n50\t\n51\t### Option 2: Class Attribute (Shared State)\n52\t\n53\t**Approach:**\n54\t```python\n55\tclass TelegramFallback:\n56\t    # Class attribute - shared across ALL instances\n57\t    _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n58\t    _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = None\n59\t    \n60\t    @classmethod\n61\t    async def get_class_lock(cls):\n62\t        if cls._first_failure_lock is None:\n63\t            cls._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n64\t        return cls._first_failure_lock\n65\t```\n66\t\n67\t**Pros:**\n68\t- State shared across all instances (if multiple were created)\n69\t- Class-level organization\n70\t\n71\t**Cons:**\n72\t- ❌ **Unnecessary complexity**: `TelegramFallback` is a singleton anyway\n73\t- ❌ **Lock initialization timing**: Class attrs init at import time, before event loop\n74\t- ❌ **Testing complexity**: Hard to reset between tests\n75\t- ❌ **Surprising behavior**: Shared state across instances is non-obvious\n76\t\n77\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity without benefit\n78\t\n79\t---\n80\t\n81\t### Option 3: Instance Variable (Per-Instance State)\n82\t\n83\t**Approach:**\n84\t```python\n85\tclass TelegramFallback:\n86\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n87\t        # Instance variables\n88\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n89\t        self._failure_count = 0\n90\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n91\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = None\n92\t        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n93\t```\n94\t\n95\t**Pros:**\n96\t- ✅ **Thread-safe with lock**: `asyncio.Lock` serializes concurrent access\n97\t- ✅ **Clear lifecycle**: Initializes when instance creates, dies when instance destroyed\n98\t- ✅ **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures one instance per app\n99\t- ✅ **Encapsulated**: State lives inside the class that uses it\n100\t- ✅ **Testable**: Each test can create a fresh instance\n101\t- ✅ **Natural reset**: Application restart = new instance = reset state\n102\t\n103\t**Cons:**\n104\t- None significant for this use case\n105\t\n106\t**Verdict:** ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Matches all requirements\n107\t\n108\t---\n109\t\n110\t### Option 4: Request Context (Per-Request State)\n111\t\n112\t**Approach:**\n113\t```python\n114\tfrom starlette.requests import Request\n115\t\n116\tasync def send_message(request: Request, chat_id, message):\n117\t    # Store in request.state\n118\t    if not hasattr(request.state, \"first_failure_logged\"):\n119\t        request.state.first_failure_logged = True\n120\t        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n121\t```\n122\t\n123\t**Pros:**\n124\t- Natural isolation per request\n125\t\n126\t**Cons:**\n127\t- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: Request-scoped means state resets EVERY request\n128\t- ❌ **Violates requirement**: Need ONE first-failure per startup, not per request\n129\t- ❌ **Impossible**: Would need to persist across requests (defeats the purpose)\n130\t\n131\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong lifecycle for this requirement\n132\t\n133\t---\n134\t\n135\t### Option 5: Database Persistence\n136\t\n137\t**Approach:**\n138\t```python\n139\t# Store in SQLite or PostgreSQL\n140\tCREATE TABLE first_failure_state (\n141\t    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,\n142\t    has_logged_first_failure BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,\n143\t    failure_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n144\t    first_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP,\n145\t    last_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP\n146\t);\n147\t```\n148\t\n149\t**Pros:**\n150\t- Survives application restarts\n151\t- Queryable for monitoring\n152\t\n153\t**Cons:**\n154\t- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: First-failure should RESET on restart, not persist\n155\t- ❌ **Performance overhead**: Database write on every failure (hot path)\n156\t- ❌ **Overkill**: No need for persistence; state is transient by design\n157\t- ❌ **Complexity**: Adds database dependency for simple boolean flag\n158\t\n159\t**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity for wrong lifecycle\n160\t\n161\t---\n162\t\n163\t## Recommended Design: Instance Variable with Singleton\n164\t\n165\t### Storage Location\n166\t\n167\t**Primary storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class\n168\t\n169\t```python\n170\t# src/telegram/fallback.py\n171\tclass TelegramFallback:\n172\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n173\t        # ... existing initialization ...\n174\t        \n175\t        # First-failure tracking state (instance variables)\n176\t        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n177\t        self._failure_count: int = 0\n178\t        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n179\t        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n180\t        \n181\t        # Thread-safety: async lock for concurrent access\n182\t        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n183\t```\n184\t\n185\t### Global Access via Singleton\n186\t\n187\tThe application uses a module-level singleton pattern:\n188\t\n189\t```python\n190\t# Global instance (module-level)\n191\t_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n192\t\n193\tdef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n194\t    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n195\t    global _telegram_fallback\n196\t    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n197\t        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n198\t    return _telegram_fallback\n199\t```\n200\t\n201\t**Why this works:**\n202\t1. **First call**: Creates the singleton instance, initializes state\n203\t2. **Subsequent calls**: Returns the same instance with preserved state\n204\t3. **Application lifetime**: One instance lives for entire app runtime\n205\t4. **Application restart**: Process exit → `_telegram_fallback = None` → fresh state on next startup\n206\t\n207\t### Initialization Timing\n208\t\n209\t| Event | What Happens | State Value |\n210\t|-------|-------------|-------------|\n211\t| **Application startup** | `get_telegram_fallback()` called for first time | All fields at defaults |\n212\t| **Module import** | `TelegramFallback` class defined | No instance yet |\n213\t| **First use** | `send_message()` → `get_telegram_fallback()` → `__init__()` | Instance created, state initialized |\n214\t| **First failure** | `_handle_send_failure()` under lock | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` |\n215\t| **Application restart** | Process exits, new process starts | Fresh instance = fresh state |\n216\t\n217\t### Reset Behavior\n218\t\n219\t| Scenario | Mechanism | Result |\n220\t|----------|-----------|--------|\n221\t| **Application restart** | Process exits, `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope | ✅ **Automatic reset** - New process = fresh instance = default state |\n222\t| **Manual reset (future)** | Call `reset_first_failure_state()` method | ✅ **Intentional reset** - Set fields back to defaults without restart |\n223\t| **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successful sends | ✅ **Conditional reset** - Reset flag when bridge recovers |\n224\t\n225\t**No persistence across restarts is intentional:**\n226\t- First-failure detection is per-startup-diagnostic, not persistent state\n227\t- Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n228\t- If bridge stays down across restarts, each restart logs one WARNING (correct behavior)\n229\t\n230\t### Thread-Safety Implementation\n231\t\n232\tThe lock protects the check-and-set sequence:\n233\t\n234\t```python\n235\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n236\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n237\t        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n238\t            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n239\t            logger.warning(...)\n240\t            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n241\t            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n242\t            self._failure_count = 1\n243\t        else:\n244\t            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n245\t            logger.debug(...)\n246\t            self._failure_count += 1\n247\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n248\t```\n249\t\n250\t**Why `asyncio.Lock`?**\n251\t- Designed for async/await contexts (FastAPI uses async)\n252\t- Non-blocking when lock is free\n253\t- Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n254\t- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast\n255\t\n256\t### State Access Patterns\n257\t\n258\t**Read access (no lock needed for simple reads):**\n259\t```python\n260\tdef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n261\t    return {\n262\t        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n263\t        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # Safe to read\n264\t        # ...\n265\t    }\n266\t```\n267\t\n268\t**Write access (always under lock):**\n269\t```python\n270\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n271\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Required\n272\t        # ... modify state ...\n273\t```\n274\t\n275\t## Why This Design Works\n276\t\n277\t### 1. Matches Application Architecture\n278\t- FastAPI is async → `asyncio.Lock` is native\n279\t- Single-process deployment → singleton pattern is natural\n280\t- Module-level services → global accessor function\n281\t\n282\t### 2. Correct Lifecycle\n283\t- **Initialization**: On first use (lazy)\n284\t- **Lifetime**: Application lifetime (singleton)\n285\t- **Reset**: On application restart (automatic)\n286\t\n287\t### 3. Thread-Safe by Design\n288\t- Lock protects the critical section\n289\t- No race conditions possible\n290\t- Clear synchronization points\n291\t\n292\t### 4. Testable\n293\t```python\n294\t# Each test gets a fresh instance\n295\tasync def test_first_failure_logging():\n296\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Fresh instance\n297\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error\")\n298\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n299\t```\n300\t\n301\t### 5. Simple and Maintainable\n302\t- All state lives in one place (the class that uses it)\n303\t- No external dependencies (databases, caches)\n304\t- Clear mental model (one instance, one state)\n305\t\n306\t## Implementation Checklist\n307\t\n308\tFor the implementation bead:\n309\t\n310\t- [ ] Add `asyncio.Lock` to `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n311\t- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently sync)\n312\t- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n313\t- [ ] Ensure all state mutations are under lock protection\n314\t- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n315\t- [ ] Add test for first-failure flag reset after restart\n316\t\n317\t## Summary\n318\t\n319\t**Storage Location:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class (singleton pattern)\n320\t\n321\t**Rationale:**\n322\t1. ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n323\t2. ✅ Clear lifecycle (init on first use, dies on process exit)\n324\t3. ✅ Natural reset on application restart\n325\t4. ✅ Accessible globally via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n326\t5. ✅ Encapsulated within the class that uses it\n327\t6. ✅ Testable (fresh instance per test)\n328\t7. ✅ Simple (no external dependencies)\n329\t\n330\t**Rejected Options:**\n331\t- Module-level variable (not thread-safe)\n332\t- Class attribute (unnecessary complexity)\n333\t- Request context (wrong lifecycle)\n334\t- Database (wrong lifecycle, overkill)\n335\t\n336\t**Reset Behavior:**\n337\t- Automatic reset on application restart (process exit → new instance)\n338\t- Future: Manual reset via method call\n339\t- Future: Recovery-based reset after N consecutive First-Failure State Storage Design\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document evaluates storage options for the first-failure tracking state in the async FastAPI application and documents the recommended approach with rationale.\n\n## Design Requirements\n\n### Functional Requirements\n1. State must track first-failure occurrence across application lifetime\n2. State must survive from application startup until shutdown\n3. Only ONE first-failure event should be detected per startup sequence\n4. State must be accessible to all `send_message()` calls\n\n### Non-Functional Requirements\n1. **Thread-safety**: Multiple concurrent async requests must not corrupt state\n2. **Performance**: Minimal overhead on the hot path (Telegram sends)\n3. **Lifecycle clarity**: Clear initialization and reset behavior\n4. **Simplicity**: Easy to understand, test, and maintain\n\n## Storage Options Evaluation\n\n### Option 1: Module-Level Variable\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\n# In src/telegram/fallback.py\n_first_failure_state = {\n    \"has_logged_first_failure\": False,\n    \"failure_count\": 0,\n    \"first_failure_timestamp\": None,\n    \"last_failure_timestamp\": None,\n}\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Simple to understand\n- Accessible from anywhere in the module\n- No instance overhead\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Not thread-safe**: Concurrent async coroutines can race on check-and-set\n- ❌ **Hard to test**: Global state pollutes tests; requires manual reset\n- ❌ **No encapsulation**: Any code can mutate state directly\n- ❌ **No lock integration**: Would need a separate global lock object\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Not thread-safe in async context\n\n---\n\n### Option 2: Class Attribute (Shared State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    # Class attribute - shared across ALL instances\n    _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n    _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = None\n    \n    @classmethod\n    async def get_class_lock(cls):\n        if cls._first_failure_lock is None:\n            cls._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n        return cls._first_failure_lock\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- State shared across all instances (if multiple were created)\n- Class-level organization\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Unnecessary complexity**: `TelegramFallback` is a singleton anyway\n- ❌ **Lock initialization timing**: Class attrs init at import time, before event loop\n- ❌ **Testing complexity**: Hard to reset between tests\n- ❌ **Surprising behavior**: Shared state across instances is non-obvious\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity without benefit\n\n---\n\n### Option 3: Instance Variable (Per-Instance State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # Instance variables\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._failure_count = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = None\n        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- ✅ **Thread-safe with lock**: `asyncio.Lock` serializes concurrent access\n- ✅ **Clear lifecycle**: Initializes when instance creates, dies when instance destroyed\n- ✅ **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures one instance per app\n- ✅ **Encapsulated**: State lives inside the class that uses it\n- ✅ **Testable**: Each test can create a fresh instance\n- ✅ **Natural reset**: Application restart = new instance = reset state\n\n**Cons:**\n- None significant for this use case\n\n**Verdict:** ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Matches all requirements\n\n---\n\n### Option 4: Request Context (Per-Request State)\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\nfrom starlette.requests import Request\n\nasync def send_message(request: Request, chat_id, message):\n    # Store in request.state\n    if not hasattr(request.state, \"first_failure_logged\"):\n        request.state.first_failure_logged = True\n        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Natural isolation per request\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: Request-scoped means state resets EVERY request\n- ❌ **Violates requirement**: Need ONE first-failure per startup, not per request\n- ❌ **Impossible**: Would need to persist across requests (defeats the purpose)\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong lifecycle for this requirement\n\n---\n\n### Option 5: Database Persistence\n\n**Approach:**\n```python\n# Store in SQLite or PostgreSQL\nCREATE TABLE first_failure_state (\n    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,\n    has_logged_first_failure BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,\n    failure_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n    first_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP,\n    last_failure_timestamp TIMESTAMP\n);\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Survives application restarts\n- Queryable for monitoring\n\n**Cons:**\n- ❌ **Wrong lifecycle**: First-failure should RESET on restart, not persist\n- ❌ **Performance overhead**: Database write on every failure (hot path)\n- ❌ **Overkill**: No need for persistence; state is transient by design\n- ❌ **Complexity**: Adds database dependency for simple boolean flag\n\n**Verdict:** ❌ **REJECTED** - Adds complexity for wrong lifecycle\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Design: Instance Variable with Singleton\n\n### Storage Location\n\n**Primary storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # ... existing initialization ...\n        \n        # First-failure tracking state (instance variables)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n        self._failure_count: int = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        \n        # Thread-safety: async lock for concurrent access\n        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n### Global Access via Singleton\n\nThe application uses a module-level singleton pattern:\n\n```python\n# Global instance (module-level)\n_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n    global _telegram_fallback\n    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    return _telegram_fallback\n```\n\n**Why this works:**\n1. **First call**: Creates the singleton instance, initializes state\n2. **Subsequent calls**: Returns the same instance with preserved state\n3. **Application lifetime**: One instance lives for entire app runtime\n4. **Application restart**: Process exit → `_telegram_fallback = None` → fresh state on next startup\n\n### Initialization Timing\n\n| Event | What Happens | State Value |\n|-------|-------------|-------------|\n| **Application startup** | `get_telegram_fallback()` called for first time | All fields at defaults |\n| **Module import** | `TelegramFallback` class defined | No instance yet |\n| **First use** | `send_message()` → `get_telegram_fallback()` → `__init__()` | Instance created, state initialized |\n| **First failure** | `_handle_send_failure()` under lock | `_has_logged_first_failure = True` |\n| **Application restart** | Process exits, new process starts | Fresh instance = fresh state |\n\n### Reset Behavior\n\n| Scenario | Mechanism | Result |\n|----------|-----------|--------|\n| **Application restart** | Process exits, `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope | ✅ **Automatic reset** - New process = fresh instance = default state |\n| **Manual reset (future)** | Call `reset_first_failure_state()` method | ✅ **Intentional reset** - Set fields back to defaults without restart |\n| **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successful sends | ✅ **Conditional reset** - Reset flag when bridge recovers |\n\n**No persistence across restarts is intentional:**\n- First-failure detection is per-startup-diagnostic, not persistent state\n- Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n- If bridge stays down across restarts, each restart logs one WARNING (correct behavior)\n\n### Thread-Safety Implementation\n\nThe lock protects the check-and-set sequence:\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(...)\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(...)\n            self._failure_count += 1\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n```\n\n**Why `asyncio.Lock`?**\n- Designed for async/await contexts (FastAPI uses async)\n- Non-blocking when lock is free\n- Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n- Minimal overhead: lock acquisition is fast\n\n### State Access Patterns\n\n**Read access (no lock needed for simple reads):**\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # Safe to read\n        # ...\n    }\n```\n\n**Write access (always under lock):**\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Required\n        # ... modify state ...\n```\n\n## Why This Design Works\n\n### 1. Matches Application Architecture\n- FastAPI is async → `asyncio.Lock` is native\n- Single-process deployment → singleton pattern is natural\n- Module-level services → global accessor function\n\n### 2. Correct Lifecycle\n- **Initialization**: On first use (lazy)\n- **Lifetime**: Application lifetime (singleton)\n- **Reset**: On application restart (automatic)\n\n### 3. Thread-Safe by Design\n- Lock protects the critical section\n- No race conditions possible\n- Clear synchronization points\n\n### 4. Testable\n```python\n# Each test gets a fresh instance\nasync def test_first_failure_logging():\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Fresh instance\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error\")\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n```\n\n### 5. Simple and Maintainable\n- All state lives in one place (the class that uses it)\n- No external dependencies (databases, caches)\n- Clear mental model (one instance, one state)\n\n## Implementation Checklist\n\nFor the implementation bead:\n\n- [ ] Add `asyncio.Lock` to `TelegramFallback.__init__()`\n- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (currently sync)\n- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n- [ ] Ensure all state mutations are under lock protection\n- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures (use `asyncio.gather()`)\n- [ ] Add test for first-failure flag reset after restart\n\n## Summary\n\n**Storage Location:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` class (singleton pattern)\n\n**Rationale:**\n1. ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n2. ✅ Clear lifecycle (init on first use, dies on process exit)\n3. ✅ Natural reset on application restart\n4. ✅ Accessible globally via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n5. ✅ Encapsulated within the class that uses it\n6. ✅ Testable (fresh instance per test)\n7. ✅ Simple (no external dependencies)\n\n**Rejected Options:**\n- Module-level variable (not thread-safe)\n- Class attribute (unnecessary complexity)\n- Request context (wrong lifecycle)\n- Database (wrong lifecycle, overkill)\n\n**Reset Behavior:**\n- Automatic reset on application restart (process exit → new instance)\n- Future: Manual reset via method call\n- Future: Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes\n","numLines":340,"startLine":1,"totalLines":340}}}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"id":"msg_2026070309034128145a2505164639","type":"message","role":"assistant","model":"glm-4.7","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"call_fd258f0d2f424f69a1af4189","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md","content":"# First-Failure State Data Structure\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document defines the data structure for tracking first-failure state after Telegram send startup in aide-de-camp. The state tracks whether the first failure after application startup has been logged, preventing log spam while ensuring visibility of bridge connectivity issues.\n\n## State Structure Definition\n\n### Location\nThe state lives as instance variables in the `TelegramFallback` class (`src/telegram/fallback.py`), accessed via the singleton `get_telegram_fallback()`.\n\n### Data Structure\n\n```python\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom typing import Optional\nimport asyncio\n\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"\n    Tracks the first-failure detection state for Telegram sends.\n    \n    This state ensures that only ONE WARNING-level log is emitted per\n    application startup when the Telegram bridge becomes unreachable,\n    while all subsequent failures are logged at DEBUG level to avoid spam.\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Primary first-failure flag\n    has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n    \"\"\"\n    Whether the first failure after startup has been logged.\n    \n    - False: No failures detected yet, or state has been reset\n    - True: First failure has been logged at WARNING level\n    \n    This is the primary flag that controls logging behavior:\n    - When False: Next failure logs at WARNING level\n    - When True: All subsequent failures log at DEBUG level\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Application restart (automatic via singleton lifecycle)\n    - Manual reset via reset_first_failure_state() method (future)\n    - Recovery-based reset after N consecutive successes (future)\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Failure tracking\n    failure_count: int = 0\n    \"\"\"\n    Total number of send failures since application startup.\n    \n    Increments on every send_message() failure.\n    Used for:\n    - Status reporting (get_bridge_status())\n    - Monitoring and metrics\n    - Potential threshold-based escalation (future)\n    \n    Does NOT reset on first-failure detection — only on application restart\n    or explicit reset.\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Timestamps\n    first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \"\"\"\n    Timestamp of the very first failure after application startup.\n    \n    Set once when the first failure occurs and never updated again\n    (until application restart or explicit reset).\n    \n    Used for:\n    - Diagnostics: \"Bridge has been down since X\"\n    - Metrics: Time-to-detection of bridge issues\n    - Correlation with other events\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Application restart (becomes None again)\n    - Manual reset (set back to None)\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \"\"\"\n    Timestamp of the most recent failure.\n    \n    Updated on every failure (not just the first).\n    \n    Used for:\n    - Status reporting: \"Last failure X seconds ago\"\n    - Health checks: Detect if failures are ongoing or intermittent\n    - Cooldown enforcement: Avoid rapid retry loops\n    \n    Reset scenarios:\n    - Never resets to None during application lifetime\n    - Always updates to the most recent failure time\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Thread safety\n    _lock: asyncio.Lock = field(default_factory=asyncio.Lock)\n    \"\"\"\n    Asyncio lock for thread-safe state mutations.\n    \n    Protects the check-and-set sequence in _handle_send_failure()\n    to prevent race conditions when multiple concurrent requests fail.\n    \n    All state mutations MUST acquire this lock first:\n    - has_logged_first_failure = True\n    - failure_count += 1\n    - first_failure_timestamp assignment\n    - last_failure_timestamp assignment\n    \n    Read operations (get_bridge_status()) do NOT require the lock\n    as they read immutable values or don't require consistency.\n    \"\"\"\n```\n\n## Integration with TelegramFallback Class\n\n### Instance Initialization\n\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        # ... existing initialization ...\n        \n        # First-failure tracking state (flat instance variables)\n        self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n        self._failure_count: int = 0\n        self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None\n        self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n### State Mutation Pattern (Thread-Safe)\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    \"\"\"\n    Handle a send failure with thread-safe first-failure detection.\n    \n    All state mutations occur under lock protection to prevent\n    race conditions from concurrent async requests.\n    \"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n            self._last_failure_logged = datetime.now()\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n            self._failure_count += 1\n            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n```\n\n### Read Access Pattern (No Lock Required)\n\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"\n    Get the current bridge status.\n    \n    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n    \"\"\"\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n    }\n```\n\n## Reset Scenarios\n\n### 1. Automatic Reset on Application Restart\n\n**Mechanism:** Process exit → `_telegram_fallback` goes out of scope → new process → fresh singleton → default state\n\n**What resets:** All fields return to defaults:\n- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n- `failure_count = 0`\n- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n- `last_failure_timestamp = None`\n\n**Why:** First-failure detection is a per-startup diagnostic. Each restart gets a fresh window to detect \"is the bridge down right now?\"\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Automatic** — No manual intervention required\n\n### 2. Manual Reset (Future Feature)\n\n**Mechanism:** Call a reset method on the singleton instance\n\n```python\nasync def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n    \"\"\"\n    Manually reset the first-failure tracking state.\n    \n    Use cases:\n    - After bridge recovery confirmation\n    - After manual bridge fix\n    - Testing scenarios\n    \n    Does NOT reset failure_count (keeps running total).\n    \"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n        # Keep failure_count and last_failure_timestamp for diagnostics\n```\n\n**What resets:**\n- `has_logged_first_failure = False`\n- `first_failure_timestamp = None`\n\n**What does NOT reset:**\n- `failure_count` (keeps running total for diagnostics)\n- `last_failure_timestamp` (most recent failure still relevant)\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Intentional** — Explicit action by operator or code\n\n### 3. Recovery-Based Reset (Future Feature)\n\n**Mechanism:** After N consecutive successful sends, reset the flag\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_success(self):\n    \"\"\"Handle a successful send (future method).\"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        if self._consecutive_success_count >= self._recovery_threshold:\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n            self._consecutive_success_count = 0\n```\n\n**What resets:** Same as manual reset\n\n**When:** After configurable number of consecutive successes (e.g., 5)\n\n**Why:** If the bridge recovers, allow a new first-failure WARNING on next degradation without requiring restart\n\n**Result:** ✅ **Conditional** — Automatic but based on recovery detection\n\n## State Lifecycle Diagram\n\n```\nApplication Startup\n    │\n    ├─► get_telegram_fallback() called\n    │   └─► TelegramFallback() instance created\n    │       └─► All state fields at defaults\n    │           ├─► has_logged_first_failure = False\n    │           ├─► failure_count = 0\n    │           ├─► first_failure_timestamp = None\n    │           └─► last_failure_timestamp = None\n    │\n    ├─► First send_message() failure occurs\n    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == False?\n    │       ├─► YES → Log WARNING, set flag\n    │       │   ├─► has_logged_first_failure = True\n    │       │   ├─► first_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │   ├─► failure_count = 1\n    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │\n    │       └─► User sees WARNING in logs\n    │\n    ├─► Subsequent failures\n    │   └─► _handle_send_failure() under lock\n    │       ├─► Check: has_logged_first_failure == True?\n    │       ├─► YES → Log DEBUG only, increment counter\n    │       │   ├─► failure_count += 1\n    │       │   └─► last_failure_timestamp = now\n    │       │\n    │       └─► User sees DEBUG logs (no WARNING spam)\n    │\n    ├─► Application restart (process exit)\n    │   └─► New process → fresh singleton → all defaults\n    │       └─► Next failure logs WARNING again\n    │\n    └─► Manual reset (future)\n        └─► reset_first_failure_state() called\n            └─► Flag resets → next failure logs WARNING\n```\n\n## Usage Examples\n\n### Example 1: Normal Operation (Bridge Up)\n\n```python\n# Startup: all fields at defaults\nfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False\nassert fallback._failure_count == 0\n\n# Send succeeds → no state changes\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Hello\")\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # unchanged\nassert fallback._failure_count == 0  # unchanged\n```\n\n### Example 2: Bridge Failure Scenario\n\n```python\n# First failure occurs\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test\")\n# → Logs WARNING (first failure detected)\n# → State updated under lock:\n#    has_logged_first_failure = True\n#    failure_count = 1\n#    first_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:30:00Z\n\n# Subsequent failures (10 more concurrent requests)\nawait asyncio.gather(*[fallback.send_message(chat_id, f\"msg{i}\") for i in range(10)])\n# → Logs 10 DEBUG messages (no WARNING spam)\n# → State updated:\n#    failure_count = 11\n#    last_failure_timestamp = 2026-07-02T14:31:45Z\n#    first_failure_timestamp = unchanged (still 14:30:00Z)\n\n# Check status\nstatus = fallback.get_bridge_status()\nassert status[\"failure_count\"] == 11\nassert status[\"has_logged_first_failure\"] == True\nassert status[\"first_failure_timestamp\"] == \"2026-07-02T14:30:00+00:00\"\n```\n\n### Example 3: Application Restart\n\n```python\n# Before restart: failure_count = 11, flag = True\n# Application restarts (process exit, new process)\n# After restart: fresh singleton\nfallback = get_telegram_fallback()\nassert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == False  # Reset!\nassert fallback._failure_count = 0  # Reset!\nassert fallback._first_failure_timestamp is None  # Reset!\n\n# Next failure logs WARNING again (new first-failure detection)\nawait fallback.send_message(chat_id, \"Test after restart\")\n# → Logs WARNING (first failure of new startup)\n```\n\n## Type Safety\n\nAll fields use proper type hints for static analysis:\n\n```python\nfrom typing import Optional\n\nhas_logged_first_failure: bool              # Always True or False\nfailure_count: int                          # Always non-negative integer\nfirst_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\nlast_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]  # datetime or None\n_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock          # Always a Lock instance\n```\n\n## Future Extensions\n\nThe state structure is designed to support future enhancements:\n\n### 1. Error Type Tracking\n\n```python\n# Add field to track error categories\nfirst_failure_error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"timeout\", \"connection_refused\", \"5xx_error\"\n```\n\n### 2. Channel-Specific Tracking (Multi-Bridge)\n\n```python\n# Add field to track which channel failed\nfirst_failure_channel: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"telegram-claude-bridge\", \"fallback-bridge\"\n```\n\n### 3. Recovery Threshold\n\n```python\n# Add fields for recovery-based reset\nconsecutive_success_count: int = 0\nrecovery_threshold: int = 5  # Reset after 5 consecutive successes\n```\n\n## Summary\n\n**State Structure:**\n- `has_logged_first_failure: bool` — Primary flag (False → True, once per startup)\n- `failure_count: int` — Total failures since startup\n- `first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of first failure\n- `last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]` — Timestamp of most recent failure\n- `_first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock` — Thread-safety for mutations\n\n**Storage:** Instance variables in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n\n**Reset Scenarios:**\n- ✅ Application restart (automatic, all fields)\n- ✅ Manual reset (future, selective fields)\n- ✅ Recovery-based reset (future, selective fields)\n\n**Thread-Safety:** All mutations protected by `asyncio.Lock`\n\n**Type Safety:** All fields properly typed for static analysis\n"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"1da7e233-edec-4de4-b15c-7384ad0b319b","uuid":"95cccacf-ea26-491f-9f87-17d5c942c218"}
